theory.canonical_statement: The temporal displacement factor $\AlphaT$ is defined by $\AlphaT = \frac{dt'}{dt}$, where $t'$ is the local time measured along a geodesic approaching $p$ and $t$ is the global time parameter.
theory.technical_interpretation: The temporal displacement factor $\AlphaT$ is defined by $\AlphaT = \frac{dt'}{dt}$, where $t'$ is the local time measured along a geodesic approaching $p$ and $t$ is the global time parameter.

equation_progression.interpretive_role: This defines the raw temporal comparison without yet committing to a singular limit.
This localizes the factor to the one regime where metric degeneracy can distort the clock relationship.
This closes the progression by tying clock distortion to the framework's internal scale rather than a purely external parametrization.

external_sources.title: Temporal Displacement Factor
external_sources.canonical_claim: The temporal displacement factor $\AlphaT$ is defined by $\AlphaT = \frac{dt'}{dt}$, where $t'$ is the local time measured along a geodesic approaching $p$ and $t$ is the global time parameter.
external_sources.exact_language: This source (Relativity lecture on Schwarzschild spacetime (MIT OpenCourseWare)) supports the physical law (gravitational time dilation) by showing that The lecture on general relativity notes that a clock at radial coordinate r in a Schwarzschild spacetime ticks slower than a clock at infinity by a factor involving the gravitational potential.
external_sources.remaining_gap: While the cited source provides partial support, a dedicated mathematical or physical proof specific to the Trawinistic framework is still required to fully establish this entry.
external_sources.source_title: Relativity lecture on Schwarzschild spacetime (MIT OpenCourseWare)
external_sources.relevance: The lecture on general relativity notes that a clock at radial coordinate r in a Schwarzschild spacetime ticks slower than a clock at infinity by a factor involving the gravitational potential. This gravitational time dilation implies a ratio \(dt'/dt\) less than one, providing a precedent for defining a temporal displacement factor based on local versus global time.

promotion_ledger.note: Append-only proof-support promotion; final physical certificate still requires review of cited source fit.
